title: Latent Degradation State via Causal Rolling Pace
summary: Recent F1 degradation modeling uses state-space ideas where latent tyre pace evolves during a stint and resets at pit stops.
rationale: Recent F1 degradation modeling uses state-space ideas where latent tyre pace evolves during a stint and resets at pit stops. The current best uses static pace deviation and degradation ratios, but not causal rolling estimates of whether degradation is accelerating.
implementation_hint: Sort by Race-Year-Driver-LapNumber and compute lagged/rolling features within current stint only: rolling mean/std/slope of LapTime_Delta, Pace_Deviation_From_Median, Cumulative_Degradation increments, and change since stint start. Shift rolling windows by one row so the current target lap is not encoded by future information.
expected_effect: Should help distinguish normal old tyres from tyres reaching a performance cliff, making next-lap pit risk more separable.
risk: If local CV is random row split, causal sequence features may look less dramatic than leakage-prone centered aggregations; sorting and stint reset logic must be correct.
